name: data-quality-frameworks
description: Implement data quality validation with Great Expectations, dbt tests, and data contracts. Use when building data quality pipelines, implementing validation rules, or establishing data contracts.
capabilities: Generate Great Expectations suites with schema, uniqueness, and validity expectations
Create dbt test configurations for column-level and relationship validation
Design data contract specifications with quality SLAs and schema definitions
Build automated data quality pipelines with checkpoint reporting
Implement custom dbt tests for business-specific validation rules
Configure alerting and notification workflows for quality failures
author: sickn33
securitySummary: This is a documentation-only skill providing markdown guides for data quality frameworks. All static analysis findings are false positives: code blocks are markdown examples not executable code, URLs are documentation references, and pattern matches on SQL terms are not actual system calls.
valueStatement: Ensure reliable data pipelines with comprehensive validation using Great Expectations, dbt tests, and data contracts. Reduce data incidents and build trust in your analytics with production-ready quality patterns.
userTitle: Build Data Quality Pipelines with Validation Frameworks
limitations: Does not execute validation code - provides configuration patterns only
Requires user to have data platform access (Snowflake, BigQuery, etc.)
Framework-specific syntax may need adaptation for your environment
Cannot validate data without connection to actual data sources

useCases.targetUser: Analytics engineers using dbt for data transformation
useCases.title: Analytics Engineer Building dbt Tests
useCases.description: Create comprehensive test suites for your dbt models with column-level validations, relationship checks, and custom business rules.

useCases.targetUser: Data platform teams managing cross-team data dependencies
useCases.title: Data Platform Team Establishing Contracts
useCases.description: Define data contracts between data producers and consumers with clear schema expectations, quality SLAs, and ownership.

useCases.targetUser: Data quality engineers building validation pipelines
useCases.title: Data Quality Lead Implementing Great Expectations
useCases.description: Deploy enterprise-grade data quality validation with expectation suites, checkpoints, and automated reporting dashboards.

promptTemplates.title: Basic Column Validation
promptTemplates.prompt: Create a Great Expectations suite for an orders table with order_id as primary key. Include expectations for not null, unique, and valid order status values (pending, processing, shipped, delivered, cancelled).
promptTemplates.scenario: Quick start with basic column-level validation

promptTemplates.title: dbt Test Configuration
promptTemplates.prompt: Generate dbt schema.yml test configurations for a customer dimension table. Include unique and not_null tests for customer_id, accepted_values for status, and a relationship test to verify referential integrity.
promptTemplates.scenario: Setting up dbt tests for dimension tables

promptTemplates.title: Data Contract Specification
promptTemplates.prompt: Design a data contract for order events streaming from an e-commerce platform. Include schema fields with types, PII classifications, quality checks using SodaCL syntax, and SLA definitions for freshness and availability.
promptTemplates.scenario: Establishing contracts between data producers and consumers

promptTemplates.title: Custom Business Rule Test
promptTemplates.prompt: Write a custom dbt test that validates order totals are consistent: subtotal + tax + shipping must equal total_amount within a 0.01 tolerance. Include the full macro and usage example.

bestPractices: Test early in the pipeline - validate source data before transformations to catch issues at ingestion
Focus on critical columns - prioritize high-impact fields over exhaustive coverage
Document each expectation with clear descriptions so team members understand the business rule
antiPatterns: Blocking production pipelines without fallback - always have a manual override path for critical data flows
Testing in isolation - validate relationships between tables, not just individual column constraints
Hardcoding thresholds - use dynamic baselines and statistical ranges that adapt to data growth

faq.question: What is the difference between Great Expectations and dbt tests?
faq.answer: Great Expectations provides rich expectation suites with detailed validation reports and Data Docs. dbt tests are SQL-based and run as part of the dbt build process. Use both: dbt for transformation-time checks, Great Expectations for deeper validation.

faq.question: How do I handle PII data in validation outputs?
faq.answer: Never include raw PII values in validation results or reports. Use aggregation, masking, or only report row counts and statistical summaries. Mark PII fields in data contracts and apply extra access controls.

faq.question: What should I do when a data quality check fails?
faq.answer: First, verify if it is a data issue or a test issue. Alert the data owner, quarantine affected data if blocking, and document the incident. Use failures to improve tests and prevent recurrence.

faq.question: How do I version data contracts?
faq.answer: Treat contracts as code: store in git, use semantic versioning, and maintain a changelog. For breaking changes, increment the major version and provide migration guidance to consumers.

faq.question: Can I run Great Expectations in my CI/CD pipeline?
faq.answer: Yes. Run lightweight expectation suites in CI to catch schema changes and critical violations. Schedule comprehensive suites separately to avoid slowing down deployments.

faq.question: What metrics should I track for data quality?
faq.answer: Track pass rate by suite, mean time to detect failures, mean time to resolution, and trend analysis over time. Monitor freshness, completeness, and validity dimensions separately.
